Did you grow up there?
Hmm?
Did you grow up there?

not.

not.
very
On not a Monday to chat with me, I can give you a little bit of background on myself and growthx.

So I lead customer operations at GrowthX.
kind of manage all of the processes and organization for what we call like the delivery org, which is like our client facing, the client facing side of our organization.
So our teams are organized into pods, and each pod serves three to five clients, and you would be kind of like the project manager.
This role is kind of like a project manager for a pod.
So you'd be managing three to five clients.
You would have one to two content managers that wouldn't report directly to you, but you would be responsible kind of for editing and checking their work output.
And then managing kind of like the long tail of deliverables that we have, like over the course of an engagement for a client.
So that also involves some client-facing work of, hey, this is where we are at the end of the week.
This is what we're hoping to get done on a Monday.
And then like, you know, call follow-ups, things like that.
And then, you know, helping edit the content.
And our, GrowthX's work is all built from an internal platform.
So all of our content is developed using...
developed
Workflows within our own platform, the content managers run those workflows, and this role would like kind of edit those workflows before sending them off or publishing them with the client.
And because we're sort of developing this platform alongside our normal like services delivery work, some of this will also be like a bit of technical troubleshooting, a feedback loop.
The platform that we're using has been around for two months now.
So it's still pretty young.
I think we have like an incredible engineering team.
And it really has, it's like, relatively stable.
But as we add more work streams and think about refining and increase refining the product and increasing efficiency, like less manual edits and more edits through like the AI workflows.
That's a lot of just like taste and giving feedback to Eng of like, this is how I would edit this.
Thank
How do we turn this into, like, more of an automated feedback loop?
So, you know, we're working on, like, agentic workflows and so helping us develop that as well.
So that's the role.
And then above you in terms of the pod structure, just the last piece is that there is a director or account strategist role, depending on the pod.
And that role manages two to three pods.
So leading, like, eight-ish accounts per person.
And that role is kind of the strategic client manager in terms of, hey, we're looking at renewals.
Let's do QBRs.
Let's think about, like, pivoting strategy, analyzing, reporting, like, higher scale, like, SEO audits, things like that.
Okay.
Makes sense.
Yes.
So that's kind of a lot of information, but that's like the way that the role would work within the team.
And so we'd be wearing two hats, really.
One is like that project management client facing hat.

Then the other one is just like that technical feedback loop and thinking kind of with a little bit of a, not that you have to know how to code, but like an engineering or product mindset to think about how we make the platform better overall so that we can scale efficiently.
Got it.
Awesome.
Well, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself and how you came to apply to this role at GrowthX?
Yeah.
So currently I'm on my own for the last two years.
I left McKinsey where I was the executive editor for AI for about six years, essentially.

Because, you know, generative AI was becoming hot, and obviously I had been in the space for a long time, and I had always thought about going off on my own, and I thought, okay, if there's any better time, it's now, and my services will be much in need, and that prediction was accurate.
And things are going well, and I would say, you know, there's a degree of overlap with what you all are doing, with what I'm doing with some of my clients.
So, you know, while I don't have my, you know, sort of tech stack built in house, I'm leveraging, you know, Claw, ChatGPT, MidJourney, to a degree, the video tools.
I haven't had to produce anything using it, but I've given some flavors of what a client might like through that.
And so, you know, I keep my eye out.
Roles that are kind of in the line of what I'm doing and where I know the ball is going.
So when this one came up, it's like, this is a great fit.
You guys are further along in your journey.
I am.
So why not join a team that's already got some legs up on me?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Super, super interesting background.
Um, so what kind of content are you producing for the clients that you're working with now?
Like, where's the focus?
Yeah, it's, um, you know, it's in the thought leadership realm.
Uh, there's a lot of articles, you know, based on experts that I interview.
Um, also some self-generated material, I guess I would call it.
Um, there was an engagement I've worked on for about a year with, uh, one of the largest consulting firms where we did this really broad.
Um,
And deep analysis of the impacts of AI and climate change on the economy and on companies' business models, et cetera, and then produced a whole set of content off of that.
I actually developed the prototype for what the interactive kind of experience would be to display some of that work in addition to working on some of the derivative articles and videos, et cetera.
So that was a big one last year.
But I also have some smaller clients, like an engineering technology firm.
They're not nearly as sophisticated, and it's kind of more demand generation type content.
Yeah.
We want to report on digital twins and their impacts in the market and where the space is going.
Or I've had one of the AI model makers was kind of.
Standing up their content function.
So I helped them create a strategy for their blogs and started creating those.
And that was an area where I was heavily using AI by my side.
Yeah.
They didn't have a lot of in-house experts.
It was more salespeople and trying to understand what they're trying to do in the market and what I could produce to help them, you know, achieve their goals.
Awesome.
Um, so how are you, how do you like juggle all of this?
How do you scale yourself?
Well, I use a bench of contractors.

So that's one way that I scale myself, um, in terms of juggling, um, you know, while in the past at McKinsey, for example, we used Airtable and, you know, the amount of stakeholders and projects I was juggling was very high.
You know, it could be 15 or more pieces of,
content at the same time.
It's not quite intense now, so I have to admit I'm using spreadsheets, because it's easier for me to keep track of things.
It's not, I wasn't feeling that I needed to invest in a platform to help me to do it.
When you're using contractors and stuff, how do you think about, like, training them and also scaling quality?
Yes.
So, I mean, I play a role similar to what your technical manager is doing.
So, I mean, I've been in the business for a while, so I kind of have my favorites of people that I've used in various capacities, you know, while I was at McKinsey and IBM.
So, I tend to, you know, towards the ones that I know I'm not going to have to provide quite as much input on.
But, really, the way that I've learned to do what I do is really just doing it.
But on paper, you know, doing it per se, doing it through my edits and being clear about why I'm making changes.
You know, if there's somebody that's needing like more whole scale, okay, this isn't working and we need to have more of a sit down.
will again be with something that we're working on and kind of talking through, okay, you know, you took this approach on the intro.
I can see why you did that, but based on what the client's telling us, you know, and what the data is showing us, I think this might be, you know, a better entry and here's why.
So just a lot of feedback that I'll give to the folks that I'm working with.
Yeah.
I'm just taking notes.
I'm just trying to think of, I mean, I feel like you covered a lot just in like very quickly, just in those.
like Okay.
you I

Yeah, I mean, I, you know, I, I want it to be obviously aligned with what I'm doing, which obviously this very much is.

Um, and then, you know, seeing.
And what he was up to, I was like, oh, okay, we're kind of going down the same path here.

So as I mentioned, just, you know, I'm one person, you know, kind of doing this and have just debated whether, you know, how much I want to scale up and kind of go in the direction of where scale is going.
And, you know, have it on the radar as a possibility.
But when there's a company that's a little further along, I say, hey, throw the hat into the ring there and get a bit of a boost right away.
And I think, you know, I'd like somewhere like this where I think I could potentially roll some of my clients in, not necessarily completely abandon them.
And that sounds like it's, there's an opportunity here for that.
And, you know, I want it to be pushing the envelope a little bit, I think, because your scale is higher than mine, I would learn a lot.
And, you
You know, working in that capacity.
So another thing is just to make sure I'm still learning.
Yeah.
You've been just learning your technology stack alone, you know, is a track to keep advancing my skills because, you know, this is where it's going.
We're all going to have AI as our partner in some way or another.
Totally.
Have you, like, worked with product teams at all before?
Yes.
Yes.
I mean, in consulting, it's, you know, the product is sort of their knowledge.
So that might be slightly different.
But even at McKinsey, we had some of our products on AI.
We had, like, had a procurement tool, for example, that would look at all of your contracts and say, okay, you know, here's where there's opportunities to renegotiate and et cetera.
So I would work with that team to see what they're trying to do.
Who are they selling into?
You know, what do these folks need to know?
Like, what are their pain points to make sure that our thought leadership was addressing that?

And then at IBM and Greenwich Associates, I mean, it was very focused on the products that they were selling and making sure that the thought leadership teams, thought leadership is aligning with what they are trying to sell.
You know, know there's some think tanky places that are a little bit more flying at a higher level, but I've tended to be in workplaces where there's a clear tie to the business value.
Yeah, yeah, totally.

This was super quick, but you've kind of checked all my boxes.
So, if I'm being honest.

I'm curious what attracted you to this role.
you.

technical part of this role actually is really interesting to me.

a bit of a, you know, push the envelope type of person.

but I didn't let that stop me.

Like, I want to know.
Wow.

So, yes, that's the trick with AI, right?
You have to be able to articulate exactly what you need.
In addition to seeing, knowing kind of the places where it can fall down or obscure content flaws a little bit.
That's, that's, I've actually come to enjoy that as well.

Drive in a little bit more to take control.

What I would say are your, what's, what do you love about the job the most right now?
And what's driving you the most crazy?
The thing I love the most is, I think we're really action oriented, like the product team and the, um, everybody wants to like move the ball forward.
Um, so I think like the team is very action oriented, which is good because what has been frustrating me is like the sort of lack of transparency in, um, process and how we're getting things done.
And so like, as we're identifying places where we can be more efficient or like save people time, um, most of the team is really like, okay.
I will change how I'm doing this thing because it's going to, you know, make it better, make it easier.
And so like sort of two-sided coin of there were a lot of inefficiencies just in the ways that we worked because our training wasn't like onboarding wasn't great.
People kind of got thrown in and then we're creating content before we had our platform.
We were doing it in like AirOps and ChatGPT, sort of just like cobbling it together.

But the reception to and willingness to like work in the platform and kind of change how they're thinking about developing content is, it's really fun to see like content people that can still think in a technical way.
And what's driving you crazy?
Well, the process piece is what's driving me crazy.
Yeah, yeah.
I think like there are so many, you know, we're, our clients were adding like a client a week, which, you know, is pretty intense.
Given that, given the resources that go into like developing all their artifacts and content and like building out the workflows, to do one kickoff a week is even pretty, like one to two is not bad.
And so like, I'm trying to find places where I can get people's time back.
And it really is like, chipping away at stone, because suddenly you're like, wait, you guys have all been doing it this way.
No, this will save you like an hour a week.
And then and so you have to just like, sort of claw back time hour by hour.
And it's like, kind of thankless, because when you look back, we've come a long way, but there's still so much more to go if we want to scale efficiently.
What are the backgrounds of your content managers generally?
Are they generalists?
Or do you try to get specialists in certain areas?
And you know, what, what is like their previous work experience generally?
Yeah.
All over the place.

I mean, we do have a couple of people like we have some like security and compliance clients and we have a couple of content managers who've really worked in that we have a couple of our managing editors have been consultants and some of them have been like content marketers.
So it's a variety.
And that's why we're like sort of walking the line on this and being like, we want both here.
But I think like, yeah, it is varied.
We've it's mostly like a writing test.
Our content managers are global.
They're all over the world.
And so, you know, they've like done a lot of like outsource contract work in terms of content generation for all kinds of companies.
And it's there's no like rhyme or reason.
And, um, but many of them have worked in, like, magazine or editorial in some capacity as well, or journalism.
Got it.
And how do you find that they adapt to your platform?
Is it finding, they find it fairly intuitive or?
Um, they are learning it.
I think, like, it, the, the UX is pretty simple and intuitive.
It's the feedback loop of the process where some people, and I mean, I think this is true of everything.
Like, if the button doesn't work, they stop trying to use the button and not be like, hey, this button didn't work.
Hey, I'll try it again next week.
They're just like, this isn't worth my time.
I will go back to the way that I did it.
Um, but for the most part, like, I think that's been the encouraging thing is the willingness.
Thank you.
To try a new way of like generating and looking at the content.
And because there is still, we have, I think, at least three points where there's like human in the loop need to review.
So their input is still incredibly important.
And there's so much to do just like, we're putting out content at five to 10 pieces a week.
So like, you kind of have to stay ahead of it on just like the planning side too, even as a content manager.
Got it.
And as the company starts to maybe transition to selling the platform itself, do you see the service side going away?
Or you see it shifting or kind of doing a little bit of both still?
Um, I mean, I think we're very far from the platform being sold as a one-off offering.
I think, like, the reason that...
We, Marcel started as a services business was he was trying to show people how to do it.
And they were like, it's really hard and annoying.
And like, I don't want to think about all the tools that I have to like link to and all that stuff.
So I think, like, there will always be that demand for just an agency to run the workflow for you.
And like, yes, you can go under the hood and see kind of like all the inputs, like, that's what people have been most excited about.
They don't want to, they still don't want to do the content generation, like when we've just like given them sneak peeks.
They want to know like, oh, which models are you using here?
And like, you know, how are you checking this?
And what kind of feedback are you giving it?
So they want more to understand, like the pieces that go into the workflow, they don't want to, nobody wants to manage the workflow.
My last question, we're pulling up against time is, what type of feedback?
everybody else.
What getting from clients at this stage?
I think the hard thing is, obviously, AI will get you to like 90-95% if the workflow is good.
Some clients want you to publish 100 pages a week or like 100 article refreshes a week, which we've done.
And they're not going to look whether there's like one or two spaces between the periods, you know.
And some clients are like, don't publish more than three articles a week because we want to read them and go through two rounds of feedback.
So it's really hard to like predict their appetite for what like and what they're prioritizing scale versus accuracy.
And like Marcel's opinion is get it out there, look for signals, then refine what's working and prune what isn't.
But some clients like can't let go of that, you know, same as.
Same as some of our team can't, you know, like there's two sides of that.
so we just try and match like, you know, if there's a CM that wants to edit every word or a managing editor that like has really been in editorial so long that they want to edit every word.
